reasoning:
The question asks whether the protest count in specific 30-day periods will exceed the 30-day average over the preceding 360 days. Event counts for political protests follow a right-skewed distribution (overdispersed), meaning the mean is significantly pulled up by major outliers (such as the massive July 2025 anti-Anwar protests and October 2025 anti-Trump/Gaza protests in Kuala Lumpur). Because the mathematical mean is higher than the median, most individual 30-day periods naturally fall below the 360-day average, giving a base rate probability strictly under 50%. 

For the first resolution date (Dec 14, window: Nov 14-Dec 13), we are already near the end of the period, with no major protest waves widely reported over the last three weeks, making it very unlikely that this specific month will surpass the elevated annual mean. For the second (Jan 2026) and third (Mar 2026) dates, there are no immediate prominent catalysts known on the horizon. Following the structural "<0.5" mathematical expectation and the rule that uncertainty increases with the horizon, estimates progressively increase towards 0.5 but remain below it (25%, 35%, 45%). Additionally, if ACLED's data continues to feature zeros due to missing or delayed reporting, strict inequality (X > Average) would fail (0 > 0 is False), which further favors NO.
